source.title: Candidate Experience Blueprint and Manager Handoff
source.author: Maya Alvarez
source.content:
Overview:

This page defines the candidate-first hiring and onboarding workflow for Redwood Inference, and provides a standard manager handoff procedure designed to reduce time-to-productivity while preserving a consistent, high-quality candidate experience. The blueprint balances speed (time-to-offer), fairness (structured evaluation), and new-hire ramp (clear owner responsibilities).

Principles:
- Candidate-first: clear timelines, transparent feedback, and a single point of contact (recruiter) for each candidate.
- Structured decisions: use calibrated rubrics and scorecards for every role.
- Fast, predictable handoffs: manager acceptance and first-week readiness must meet SLAs below.
- Continuous improvement: capture candidate NPS and iterate quarterly.

Scope:
- Applies to all full-time and long-term contract technical and non-technical hires across Redwood Inference (hosted, dedicated, private teams).
- Excludes executive-level (C-suite) hiring and external agency-managed searches (see exceptions).

Owner and escalation:
- Document owner: People Operations (Recruiting) — lead: Maya Alvarez.
- Day-to-day owner for manager handoff: hiring manager.
- Escalation path: recruiter -> TA lead -> Head of People -> CEO (for approvals >$300k comp or special exceptions).

Target SLAs (company-wide):
- First recruiter outreach to candidate: within 48 business hours of inbound interest.
- Interview loop scheduled: within 10 business days of phone screen.
- Offer extended: within 5 business days of final onsite/loop.
- Manager acceptance of handoff packet: within 48 hours of offer acceptance.

Metrics to track (weekly/quarterly):
- Time-to-offer (median, by role band).
- Offer acceptance rate (by role and region).
- Candidate NPS (post-decision survey).
- First-90-day ramp score (manager-reported).

Candidate journey (high level):
1) Sourcing & prescreen
   - Recruiter creates JD in Greenhouse using the official template (JD_TEMPLATE_V3) and links the role to the hiring manager.
   - Passive candidate outreach must include: role summary, top-3 differentiators (team, mission, comp band), and a one-line ask.

2) Phone screen (30–45 min)
   - Objective: role fit and flag-check. Use phone-screen scorecard.
   - Recruiter documents notes in Greenhouse and sets next steps within 24 hours.

3) Interview loop (onsite or virtual, 2–4 interviewers)
   - Use a standardized loop: problem-solving (technical/role), system design or role simulation, culture & collaboration, and compensation/expectations alignment.
   - Each interviewer completes a calibrated scorecard in Greenhouse within 24 hours.

4) Decision & debrief
   - Hiring manager convenes decision debrief within 48 hours. Hiring committee rubric: pass/fail + confidence score (1–5).
   - If >2 conflicting scores, escalate to hiring manager + TA lead for alignment.

5) Offer & negotiation
   - Compensation team drafts offer using COMP_TOOL_v2. All offers routed through People Ops for compliance. Standard offer SLA = 5 business days.

6) Acceptance & pre-boarding
   - Upon acceptance: Recruiter triggers Pre-boarding Packet (see template link). Packet includes first-week agenda, equipment checklist, and required HR forms.
   - Manager must confirm onboarding slot and team introduction meeting within 48 hours.

Manager Handoff: required materials and checklist
- Purpose: ensure managers receive a complete, actionable packet so new hires start productive work in week 1 and have clear success criteria for 30/60/90 days.

Handoff SLA: manager acknowledges receipt within 48 hours and completes the Manager Readiness Checklist within 5 business days.

Manager Handoff Packet (deliverables)
- Candidate summary (TL;DR): role, key strengths, interview highlights, known areas to support.
- Offer metadata: start date, compensation, relocation or sign-on details, visa sponsorship notes.
- Interview scorecards (download PDF from Greenhouse).
- Confirmed first-week plan (see First-Week Template below).
- Onboarding tasks assigned in Workday/Notion: equipment request, access requests, security training schedule.
- Org chart snapshot with first-level mentors and primary buddy.

Manager Readiness Checklist (table)
| Item | Owner | Target completion | Evidence |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Acknowledge candidate packet in Greenhouse | Manager | 48 hours | Greenhouse acknowledgment flag |
| Confirm Day 1 agenda | Manager | 72 hours | Calendar invite for Day 1 meeting |
| Assign buddy | Manager | 72 hours | Buddy assigned in Notion profile |
| Request equipment | Manager / IT | 5 days before start | IT ticket # or email confirmation |
| Create first-week goals | Manager | 5 days before start | Goals posted to Notion / Manager notes |
| Add 30/60/90 success metrics | Manager | 5 days before start | Goals in Performance template |

First-Week Template (example agenda)
- Day 0 (evening before): Welcome email from manager + team intro thread.
- Day 1: HR orientation (60 min), Manager intro & team lunch (90 min), Tools + access walkthrough (60 min).
- Day 2: Product orientation and shadowing sessions.
- Day 3: Small starter task assigned; first-week check-in (30 min).
- Day 4: Cross-functional meet-and-greets.
- Day 5: 30/60/90 goal review and expectations sync; end-of-week feedback survey.

Sample manager message (copy-paste)
Hi <New hire>,

Welcome to Redwood Inference — excited to have you join the <team> on <start date>! On day one we’ll meet in the main conference room (or Zoom) at 10:00 AM. I’ve scheduled a 90-minute intro and a team lunch. Your buddy will be <name> and will help you with day-to-day tooling. Please complete the New Hire Forms sent by People Ops before the start date.

Looking forward to your first week — please reach out if you have any questions.

Best,
<Manager name>

Ramp & performance assessment (30/60/90)
- Managers should set measurable objectives for 30/60/90 days using the Performance Template in Notion. Examples: first PR merged, first production inference experiment run, first customer demo.
- Managers complete a formal ramp assessment at day 30 and 90 in the People Ops portal. A low ramp score (<2/5) triggers a coaching plan and weekly follow-ups.

Performance and feedback cadence for new hires:
- Weekly 1:1s during first 90 days.
- Formal check-ins at 30 and 90 days with People Ops in attendance for all ICs level 3 and above.
- Feedback collected via manager and peer notes; red flags (missed deliverables, disengagement) escalate to People Ops within 10 business days.

Special cases and exceptions:
- International hires: ensure visa and relocation tasks are added to the handoff packet; allow an extra 2 weeks for onboarding SLAs.
- Contract-to-hire transitions: include contract history and performance notes; require a formal conversion decision 2 weeks before contract end.
- Senior leadership hires: engage Executive Recruiting and Legal early; use Executive Handoff Packet instead of standard packet.

Tools & templates (links)
- Greenhouse role and scorecard templates: GH -> Templates -> Redwood JDs.
- Offer generation: internal COMP_TOOL_v2 (People Ops drive).
- Pre-boarding packet template: Notion -> People Ops -> Pre-boarding.
- Performance 30/60/90 template: Notion -> People Ops -> Performance Templates.

Continuous improvement:
- Quarterly retro: People Ops collects candidate NPS, hiring manager feedback, and time-to-offer metrics. Action items are added to the Recruiting roadmap.
- A/B experiments: test variations of first-week agendas and buddy pairings; measure first-90-day ramp delta.

Appendix: quick checklists and shortcuts
- Recruiter quick checklist (for each active role): create JD, confirm comp band, attach scorecards, schedule interviewers, set debrief meeting (all in Greenhouse).
- Manager quick checklist: review packet, schedule Day 1, assign buddy, set starter task.
- Escalation email template (copy-paste):
Subject: Urgent - Handoff SLA breach for <candidate name>
Body: Hi <TA Lead>,
We have a handoff SLA breach for <candidate>. Manager acknowledgment missing after 72 hours. Candidate start date is <date>. Please advise next steps.

Related policies and reading:
- Equity interview calibration (see /confluence/people-ops/interview-calibration-and-hiring-scorecard-guidelines-2025.json).
- Visa & relocation checklist (People Ops internal drive).

Contact:
- Recruiting lead: maya.alvarez@redwood-inference.example.com
- People Ops escalation: ta-lead@redwood-inference.example.com

Last reviewed: 2029-03-02 (People Ops)

source.title: shadow-rotation-and-talent-braiding-playbook-2027
source.author: Priya Raman
source.content:
Summary:
This playbook defines Redwood's Shadow Rotation & Talent Braiding program: a structured, time-boxed approach to accelerate new hire ramp, cross-team knowledge transfer, and internal mobility through short rotations, paired mentorship, and explicit success metrics. The program is intended for early-career hires, role-transfers, and high-potential talent preparing for expanded scope.

Goals and Success Metrics:
- Reduce median time-to-first-meaningful-PR by 25% within the first 90 days for participating engineers.
- Increase cross-team incident familiarity score by 30% for rotated staff (measured in post-rotation evals).
- Surface at least 10 internal mobility matches per quarter through the rotation pipeline.

Scope:
- Applies to: engineering, product, and platform ICs and managers entering a new domain or team at Redwood.
- Excludes: short-term contractors (<30 days) and individual contributors on active PIP plans.
- Pilot cadence: run at least one cycle per quarter per function during initial rollout.

High-level Program Components:
1) Rotation Blocks: 2–6 week focused engagements on host teams with clear objectives.
2) Talent Braiding: pairing the rotating employee with a host-team mentor and an originating-team buddy to ensure bidirectional learning.
3) Defined Success Criteria: tangible artifacts to evaluate (PRs, runbook edits, dashboard widgets, support tickets resolved).
4) Feedback & Calibration: standard evaluation forms and a calibration committee review after each cycle.

Roles & Responsibilities:
- Participant (rotating employee): complete the rotation objectives, update learning log, and present a short demo at handback.
- Home Manager: nominate participants, approve rotation time, and own career conversation post-rotation.
- Host Manager: define objectives, assign a mentor, and provide day-to-day guidance.
- Mentor (host team): technical and operational guide; primary feedback provider.
- Buddy (home team): supports context handover and reintegration.
- Program Lead (People Ops): coordinates slots, monitors metrics, and runs calibration sessions.

Eligibility and Timeframes (table):
| Participant Type | Typical Rotation Length | Allowed per Year | Notes |
|------------------|-------------------------|------------------|-------|
| New engineering hire (domain change) | 2–4 weeks | 1 | Should occur within first 60 days of hire |
| IC requesting lateral exposure | 3–6 weeks | 2 | Requires manager sign-off |
| Manager-level cross-functional shadow | 4–6 weeks | 1 | Focus on leadership and stakeholder mapping |
| Product manager rotation into infra | 3–4 weeks | 2 | Include objectives around telemetry and costs |

Design Principles:
- Time-boxed: rotations have fixed durations and clearly defined milestones.
- Measurable outcomes: every rotation ends with at least one measurable deliverable.
- Reciprocity: knowledge transfer flows both ways; the host team gets documented improvements.
- Low-friction access: participants get temporary access via Access Service tooling; provisioning time target is 48 hours.
- Psychological safety: mentors and managers sign an agreement to support learning-first feedback.

Rotation Request and Approval Flow (numbered procedure):
1. Nomination: Home manager or participant submits a Rotation Request via People Portal (form: rotation-request-v2).
2. Host Intake: Host manager reviews objectives and assigns a mentor within 5 business days.
3. Access & Onboarding: People Ops triggers temporary access (Okta groups + IDP SSO) and schedules a 1-hour domain orientation. Target: complete within 48 hours of approval.
4. Kickoff: 30-minute kickoff with Home Manager, Host Manager, Mentor, and Participant to align expectations.
5. Weekly checkpoints: Mentor and Participant run a 30-minute check-in; status recorded in the learning log template.
6. Handback: Participant delivers a 20-minute demo + 1-page handback note; Host Manager completes evaluation form.
7. Calibration and Outcomes: People Ops runs an outcomes review and records the outputs in the rotation ledger. Home Manager schedules next career conversation.

Rotation Objectives Template (example checklist):
- Objective 1: Ship or contribute to a scoped PR that fixes bug or adds an integration (owner: participant).
- Objective 2: Update or create a one-page runbook for the feature area (owner: participant + mentor).
- Objective 3: Identify 1 measurable telemetry improvement or cost optimization opportunity (owner: participant).
- Objective 4: Deliver a demo and handback document (owner: participant).

Access Matrix & Security Controls (table):
| Asset Type | Access Level | Provisioning Owner | Expiry | Notes |
|------------|--------------|--------------------|--------|-------|
| Production read-only dashboards | Read-only | Host Manager + Access Service | rotation end + 1 day | Temporary tokens via IDP |
| Non-prod clusters | Build & deploy | Infrastructure team | rotation end | Review by infra before provisioning |
| Code repo (host team) | Write (scoped) | Repo admin | PR merged or rotation end | Scoped branch access only |

Evaluation and Feedback:
- Standardized forms:
  - Mentor Evaluation (technical, collaboration, learning velocity)
  - Participant Self-Reflection (what went well, gaps, next steps)
  - Host Team Impact Form (artifacts, runbook updates, measurable improvements)
- Scoring rubric (0–4 scale): technical contribution, autonomy, communication, and operational readiness.
- Outcomes: Pass / Partial / Defer. Partial outcomes must include a 30-day remediation plan.

Measurement and Reporting:
- People Ops will publish quarterly KPIs: median ramp time for participants vs. non-participants, number of internal hires resulting from rotations, participant NPS.
- Weekly program dashboard fields: open slots, provisioning SLA, active rotations, and blockers.

Sample Rotation Schedule (ASCII table):
| Week | Activity | Owner | Deliverable |
|------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 0 | Kickoff + access provisioning | Host Manager / People Ops | Kickoff notes, access granted |
| 1 | Contribute first PR, runbook draft | Participant + Mentor | PR + runbook draft |
| 2 | Telemetry discovery, iterate on PR | Participant | Telemetry doc, updated PR |
| 3 | Finalize PR, demo prep | Participant | Merged PR or reviewed PR |

Risk & Mitigations:
- Risk: Host team disruption due to mentoring load. Mitigation: cap rotations per host to 1 active rotatee per 6 engineers and offer mentor credit hours as part of manager performance reviews.
- Risk: Security exposure from temporary access. Mitigation: use time-bound credentials and automated revocation; audit trail kept in Access Service.

- Pre-rotation: approve objectives, assign mentor, provision access, schedule orientation.
- During: weekly learning log updates, weekly mentor checkpoints, triage open items.

Summary:
This playbook defines Redwood's Shadow Rotation & Talent Braiding program: a structured, time-boxed approach to accelerate new hire ramp, cross-team knowledge transfer, and internal mobility through short rotations, paired mentorship, and explicit success metrics. The program is intended for early-career hires, role-transfers, and high-potential talent preparing for expanded scope.

Goals and Success Metrics:
- Reduce median time-to-first-meaningful-PR by 25% within the first 90 days for participating engineers.
- Increase cross-team incident familiarity score by 30% for rotated staff (measured in post-rotation evals).
- Surface at least 10 internal mobility matches per quarter through the rotation pipeline.

Scope:
- Applies to: engineering, product, and platform ICs and managers entering a new domain or team at Redwood.
- Excludes: short-term contractors (<30 days) and individual contributors on active PIP plans.
- Pilot cadence: run at least one cycle per quarter per function during initial rollout.

High-level Program Components:
1) Rotation Blocks: 2–6 week focused engagements on host teams with clear objectives.
2) Talent Braiding: pairing the rotating employee with a host-team mentor and an originating-team buddy to ensure bidirectional learning.
3) Defined Success Criteria: tangible artifacts to evaluate (PRs, runbook edits, dashboard widgets, support tickets resolved).
4) Feedback & Calibration: standard evaluation forms and a calibration committee review after each cycle.

Roles & Responsibilities:
- Participant (rotating employee): complete the rotation objectives, update learning log, and present a short demo at handback.
- Home Manager: nominate participants, approve rotation time, and own career conversation post-rotation.
- Host Manager: define objectives, assign a mentor, and provide day-to-day guidance.
- Mentor (host team): technical and operational guide; primary feedback provider.
- Buddy (home team): supports context handover and reintegration.
- Program Lead (People Ops): coordinates slots, monitors metrics, and runs calibration sessions.

Eligibility and Timeframes (table):
| Participant Type | Typical Rotation Length | Allowed per Year | Notes |
|------------------|-------------------------|------------------|-------|
| New engineering hire (domain change) | 2–4 weeks | 1 | Should occur within first 60 days of hire |
| IC requesting lateral exposure | 3–6 weeks | 2 | Requires manager sign-off |
| Manager-level cross-functional shadow | 4–6 weeks | 1 | Focus on leadership and stakeholder mapping |
| Product manager rotation into infra | 3–4 weeks | 2 | Include objectives around telemetry and costs |

Design Principles:
- Time-boxed: rotations have fixed durations and clearly defined milestones.
- Measurable outcomes: every rotation ends with at least one measurable deliverable.
- Reciprocity: knowledge transfer flows both ways; the host team gets documented improvements.
- Low-friction access: participants get temporary access via Access Service tooling; provisioning time target is 48 hours.
- Psychological safety: mentors and managers sign an agreement to support learning-first feedback.

Rotation Request and Approval Flow (numbered procedure):
1. Nomination: Home manager or participant submits a Rotation Request via People Portal (form: rotation-request-v2).
2. Host Intake: Host manager reviews objectives and assigns a mentor within 5 business days.
3. Access & Onboarding: People Ops triggers temporary access (Okta groups + IDP SSO) and schedules a 1-hour domain orientation. Target: complete within 48 hours of approval.
4. Kickoff: 30-minute kickoff with Home Manager, Host Manager, Mentor, and Participant to align expectations.
5. Weekly checkpoints: Mentor and Participant run a 30-minute check-in; status recorded in the learning log template.
6. Handback: Participant delivers a 20-minute demo + 1-page handback note; Host Manager completes evaluation form.
7. Calibration and Outcomes: People Ops runs an outcomes review and records the outputs in the rotation ledger. Home Manager schedules next career conversation.

Rotation Objectives Template (example checklist):
- Objective 1: Ship or contribute to a scoped PR that fixes bug or adds an integration (owner: participant).
- Objective 2: Update or create a one-page runbook for the feature area (owner: participant + mentor).
- Objective 3: Identify 1 measurable telemetry improvement or cost optimization opportunity (owner: participant).
- Objective 4: Deliver a demo and handback document (owner: participant).

Access Matrix & Security Controls (table):
| Asset Type | Access Level | Provisioning Owner | Expiry | Notes |
|------------|--------------|--------------------|--------|-------|
| Production read-only dashboards | Read-only | Host Manager + Access Service | rotation end + 1 day | Temporary tokens via IDP |
| Non-prod clusters | Build & deploy | Infrastructure team | rotation end | Review by infra before provisioning |
| Code repo (host team) | Write (scoped) | Repo admin | PR merged or rotation end | Scoped branch access only |

Evaluation and Feedback:
- Standardized forms:
  - Mentor Evaluation (technical, collaboration, learning velocity)
  - Participant Self-Reflection (what went well, gaps, next steps)
  - Host Team Impact Form (artifacts, runbook updates, measurable improvements)
- Scoring rubric (0–4 scale): technical contribution, autonomy, communication, and operational readiness.
- Outcomes: Pass / Partial / Defer. Partial outcomes must include a 30-day remediation plan.

Measurement and Reporting:
- People Ops will publish quarterly KPIs: median ramp time for participants vs. non-participants, number of internal hires resulting from rotations, participant NPS.
- Weekly program dashboard fields: open slots, provisioning SLA, active rotations, and blockers.

Sample Rotation Schedule (ASCII table):
| Week | Activity | Owner | Deliverable |
|------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 0 | Kickoff + access provisioning | Host Manager / People Ops | Kickoff notes, access granted |
| 1 | Contribute first PR, runbook draft | Participant + Mentor | PR + runbook draft |
| 2 | Telemetry discovery, iterate on PR | Participant | Telemetry doc, updated PR |
| 3 | Finalize PR, demo prep | Participant | Merged PR or reviewed PR |
| 4 | Handback, evaluation, career sync | Home Manager / Host Manager | Handback note + eval forms |

Risk & Mitigations:
- Risk: Host team disruption due to mentoring load. Mitigation: cap rotations per host to 1 active rotatee per 6 engineers and offer mentor credit hours as part of manager performance reviews.
- Risk: Security exposure from temporary access. Mitigation: use time-bound credentials and automated revocation; audit trail kept in Access Service.
- Risk: Varying quality of mentorship. Mitigation: mentor training micro-module required for all mentors; mentor sign-off required before rotation begins.

Onboarding & Reintegration Checklist (bulleted):
- Pre-rotation: approve objectives, assign mentor, provision access, schedule orientation.
- During: weekly learning log updates, weekly mentor checkpoints, triage open items.
- Post-rotation: demo + handback note, host evaluation, home manager career conversation, record artifacts in team wiki and rotation ledger.

Incentives and Recognition:
- Mentors receive 8 hours of mentorship credit that can be allocated to performance conversations or time-banked for hiring bonus considerations.
- Rotations that result in a successful internal hire are acknowledged in the quarterly People Ops newsletter and considered in calibration.

Tooling and Templates (links/reference IDs):
- Rotation Request Form: People Portal -> /forms/rotation-request-v2
- Learning Log Template: /templates/rotation-learning-log-v1
- Mentor Evaluation Form: /templates/mentor-eval-v1
- Rotation Ledger (internal): /dashboards/rotation-ledger (People Ops)

Rollout Plan & Timeline:
- Q2 (pilot): 3 host teams in Eng Infra, Product Ops, and Platform. Measure provisioning SLA and participant ramp.
- Q3: expand to additional product engineering squads. Run mentor training cohort.
- Q4: program scale with monthly intake and automation for access provisioning.

Appendix: Example Handback Note (short):
- Summary: 3-week rotation focused on observability improvements for the feature X.
- Artifacts produced: PR #347 (adds dashboard), runbook page /runbooks/feature-x, telemetry doc.
- Key learnings: Observability gaps in sampling, suggested follow-ups: add sampling adjustments and set alert thresholds.
- Next steps: Home manager to pair with participant on follow-up tickets; Host manager to tag volunteers for ongoing work.

Related pages:
- /people-ops/onboarding-immersion-rotation-playbook-2026
- /eng-infra/runbooks/provisioning-playbook

Owner & Contact:
- Program Lead: Priya Raman (people-ops@redwood-inference.com)
- Operational questions: People Ops rotation inbox (rotations@redwood-inference.com)

Change Log:
- 2027-03-15: Created (Priya Raman).
- 2027-07-11: Pilot adjustments: added mentor training requirement and provisioning SLA. (Ethan Park).
- 2028-01-10: Updated incentives and added evaluation rubric details. (Laura Mendes).

This playbook is intended to be a living document. For feedback or proposed edits, contact the Program Lead and raise a suggestion via the People Portal change request flow.

source.title: New Engineer Autonomy and Success Blueprint
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary
-------
This blueprint defines the end-to-end process to move a newly hired engineer from first-day orientation to independent contributor with measurable autonomy and impact. It covers the hiring handoff, staged onboarding milestones (30/60/90/180 days), manager and buddy responsibilities, performance and feedback cadence, role-readiness rubric, accommodations, and operational templates to standardize successful activation across teams.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all software engineering hires (IC levels L1–L6) across hosted, dedicated, and private product teams.
- Excludes contractor-only engagements where a separate contractor activation process exists (see related contractor playbook).
- Intended for hiring managers, people-ops partners, technical buddies, and platform/infra owners who contribute to onboarding.

Objectives and success criteria
-----------------------------
1. Reduce average time-to-autonomy (TTA) measured as the median days until a new hire independently completes a deliverable without step-by-step assistance. Target: 90 days for mid-level hires, 180 for senior cross-team roles.
2. Increase first-quarter calibration accuracy: managers' readiness assessment aligned with peer-review and code-review signals > 80%.
3. Maintain new hire satisfaction NPS >= +40 at day 30 and day 90 surveys.

How this document is organized
-----------------------------
1. Hiring-to-Day-0 handoff checklist
2. 30/60/90/180 milestone plan (including acceptance criteria)
3. Manager and buddy responsibilities
4. Onboarding task checklist (technical and non-technical)
5. Performance & feedback cadence and templates
6. Role-readiness rubric and evidence guidelines
7. Accommodations, escalations, and remediation paths
8. Metrics, dashboards, and quarterly review triggers
9. Templates and links (code access, security training, payroll, equipment)

1) Hiring-to-Day-0 handoff checklist
-----------------------------------
Manager responsibilities (pre-start, due at least 5 working days before start):
- Confirm offer details, start date, and first-week agenda with candidate.
- Submit equipment and access requests: laptop spec, peripheral needs, VPN, KMS access, cloud billing account assignment.
- Create a 30-day plan draft and share it with people-ops and the assigned buddy.
- Add new-hire to team calendar invites for orientation, team retro, and product demo.
- Assign a technical buddy and schedule a 90-minute Day-1 deep-dive.

People-ops responsibilities (pre-start):
- Send welcome packet (logistics, benefits links, tax forms) and first-week checklist.
- Kick off security/compliance training enrollment (must-complete by day 7).
- Coordinate relocation or remote-stipend logistics if applicable.

Technical buddy responsibilities (pre-start):
- Verify onboarding repo links, runbook access, and recommended reading list are up to date.
- Prepare starter ticket (low-risk bug or doc edit) for day 7–14.

2) 30/60/90/180 milestone plan and acceptance criteria
-----------------------------------------------------
Milestone: Day 0–30 (Orientation & Setup)
- Goals:
  - Full network and repo access, CI credentials, and IDE toolchain configured.
  - Complete security/compliance training and basic infra walkthrough.
  - Submit first non-production PR (doc, small bugfix).
- Acceptance criteria:
  - All required accounts active and verified in onboarding tracker.
  - Buddy confirms first PR merged or constructive feedback given.
  - Day-30 pulse survey returned.

Milestone: Day 31–60 (Contribution & Collaboration)
- Goals:
  - Independently own one small user-facing route or feature bug.
  - Present a short demo at a team sync.
  - First 1:1 reflection with manager resulting in a development plan.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Demonstrated pipeline from local dev to CI to staging.
  - Code review feedback incorporated and peer-reviewed by two teammates.

Milestone: Day 61–90 (Autonomy & Impact)
- Goals:
  - Deliver a scoped feature or optimization from spec to merged PR with ownership of rollout plan.
  - Begin pairing on cross-team work or integrations.
  - Establish recurring 1:1 rhythm and career conversation.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Manager and buddy independently endorse "role-ready" for routine tasks using the role-readiness rubric.
  - Positive signals in telemetry (reduced review turnaround, increased commit independence).

Milestone: Day 91–180 (Cross-team scope & Leadership foundations)
- Goals:
  - Lead a small cross-functional initiative or reduce a documented production pain point by >25% in latency/cost/error rate.
  - Mentor a new onboarder or run a knowledge-sharing session.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Evidence of broader ownership in roadmap tickets and architecture notes.
  - Promotion readiness floor met for senior hires (if applicable).

3) Manager and buddy responsibilities (detailed)
------------------------------------------------
Manager (ongoing):
- Create and update the 30/60/90/180 plan within the team onboarding template.
- Hold weekly 1:1s (day 7–90 recommended cadence) aimed at removing blockers and calibrating expectations.
- Run a formal 90-day readout: document outcomes, learning, and next-phase goals.
- Provide immediate, concrete feedback within 48 hours of any missed acceptance criteria.

Buddy (ongoing):
- Serve as the first escalation for day-to-day technical questions for first 90 days.
- Provide guided code reviews and pair-program weekly for first 30 days.
- Validate completion of checklist items and mark acceptance criteria as met in the onboarding tracker.

4) Onboarding task checklist (to be tracked in the onboarding tracker)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Technical access:
- Cloud console access (AWS/GCP) – IAM role noted
- Repo access (git, monorepo path), commit signing configured
- CI credentials and developer sandbox created
- Local stack and infrastructure automation run (script: scripts/bootstrap.sh)

Security & compliance:
- Complete security training module 1 and 2 (enrollment link)
- Sign data handling and NDA acknowledgements
- Register device with endpoint management (MDM/HSM)

Operational & product:
- Join product channel(s), read product PRD and current roadmap summary
- Shadow customer-support queue for two 2-hour blocks during week 2

People & culture:
- Schedule intro 1:1s with cross-functional partners (SRE, Product, Sales)
- Review company values and code of conduct; complete the culture exercise in the welcome packet

5) Performance & feedback cadence and templates
-----------------------------------------------
Recommended cadence:
- Weekly manager 1:1s (first 90 days direct focus on ramp impediments)
- Bi-weekly buddy syncs (peer feedback and pairing)
- Day-30 and Day-90 structured pulse surveys (people-ops) with automated reminders
- 90-day readout meeting: manager, buddy, people-ops partner present

Feedback templates (short form):
- "Observed behavior": [concrete example]
- "Impact": [user/product/infra/people impact]
- "Suggested next step": [concrete, time-bound suggestion]

Calibrations:
- At day 90, the manager completes a role-readiness rubric and shares it with hiring manager and people-ops for calibration when cross-team work was expected.

6) Role-readiness rubric and evidence guidelines
------------------------------------------------
Rubric dimensions (each rated 1–5):
- Technical competence (coding, architecture patterns)
- Independent ownership (can deliver scoped pieces end-to-end)
- Collaboration (PR reviews, cross-team communication)
- Reliability (on-call readiness, SLA understanding)
- Growth mindset (incorporates feedback, learning cadence)

Evidence examples (accepted artifacts):
- Merged PRs with clear changelogs and test coverage
- Architecture notes or ADR contributions
- Post-release monitoring dashboards showing reduced error trends
- Peer feedback excerpts from 2+ teammates

7) Accommodations, escalations, and remediation paths
-----------------------------------------------------
Reasonable accommodations:
- People-ops coordinates accommodations for disability access, adjusted start dates, or equipment needs. Managers must not ask for medical details; direct to people-ops.

Escalation levels:
- Level 1 (team-level): manager + buddy; target resolution 5 working days.
- Level 2 (people-ops involvement): People-ops partner coordinates and documents adjustments; target 10 working days.
- Level 3 (formal performance remediation): Initiate Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) per performance policy with HR/legal review; see templates in related pages.

8) Metrics, dashboards, and quarterly review triggers
----------------------------------------------------
Primary metrics to track (dashboard maintained by people-ops):
- Time-to-autonomy distribution (median, 75th percentile) broken down by role and seniority
- Day-30 and Day-90 NPS and qualitative themes
- Onboarding checklist completion rate by day 7 and day 30
- New-hire bug/rollback rate for first 90 days (normalized by team size)

Quarterly triggers:
- If median TTA > target for two consecutive quarters for a hiring cohort, trigger a cross-functional onboarding retro and remediation plan.

9) Templates and links (appendix)
----------------------------------
- 30/60/90 plan template: /confluence/templates/30-60-90-onboarding-template
- Onboarding tracker: internal spreadsheet link (private)
- New-hire PR starter ticket template: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATES/onboarding.md
- Security training enrollment: /learning/security/mandatory
- Equipment request form: /it/equipment-requests

Appendix A: Example 90-day readout agenda
-----------------------------------------
1. 5 min - Manager opening: objectives and current state
2. 10 min - New hire demo: show what was built or improved
3. 10 min - Buddy observations and evidence (PRs, dashboards)
4. 15 min - Manager and new-hire calibration on next-phase goals
5. 10 min - People-ops notes and final acceptance status ("On track", "Needs support", "Remediation")
6. Action items and owners (published to the onboarding tracker)

Appendix B: Quick-start checklist for remote hires (first 72 hours)
------------------------------------------------------------------
- Day 0: Welcome packet, account activation, schedule 1:1 with manager and buddy (30m each)
- Day 1: Orientation session, MDM enrollment, run bootstrap.sh with buddy over Zoom
- Day 2–3: Take starter ticket, join product demo, complete security module 1

Ownership and maintenance
------------------------
- Primary owner: people-ops (onboarding program) – Ava Chen
- Secondary owners: hiring manager and technical buddy assigned per hire
- Review cadence: this blueprint must be reviewed annually or after any major changes to the platform onboarding flow (e.g., new IAM model, VPC onboarding changes).

Feedback on this blueprint
-------------------------

title: New Engineer Autonomy and Success Blueprint
preview:
Summary
-------
This blueprint defines the end-to-end process to move a newly hired engineer from first-day orientation to independent contributor with measurable autonomy and impact. It covers the hiring handoff, staged onboarding milestones (30/60/90/180 days), manager and buddy responsibilities, performance and feedback cadence, role-readiness rubric, accommodations, and operational templates to standardize successful activation across teams.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all software engineering hires (IC levels L1–L6) across hosted, dedicated, and private product teams.
- Excludes contractor-only engagements where a separate contractor activation process exists (see related contractor playbook).
- Intended for hiring managers, people-ops partners, technical buddies, and platform/infra owners who contribute to onboarding.

Objectives and success criteria
-----------------------------
1. Reduce average time-to-autonomy (TTA) measured as the median days until a new hire independently completes a deliverable without step-by-step assistance. Target: 90 days for mid-level hires, 180 for senior cross-team roles.
2. Increase first-quarter calibration accuracy: managers' readiness assessment aligned with peer-review and code-review signals > 80%.
3. Maintain new hire satisfaction NPS >= +40 at day 30 and day 90 surveys.

How this document is organized
-----------------------------
1. Hiring-to-Day-0 handoff checklist
2. 30/60/90/180 milestone plan (including acceptance criteria)
3. Manager and buddy responsibilities
4. Onboarding task checklist (technical and non-technical)
5. Performance & feedback cadence and templates
6. Role-readiness rubric and evidence guidelines
7. Accommodations, escalations, and remediation paths
8. Metrics, dashboards, and quarterly review triggers
9. Templates and links (code access, security training, payroll, equipment)

1) Hiring-to-Day-0 handoff checklist
-----------------------------------
Manager responsibilities (pre-start, due at least 5 working days before start):
- Confirm offer details, start date, and first-week agenda with candidate.
- Submit equipment and access requests: laptop spec, peripheral needs, VPN, KMS access, cloud billing account assignment.
- Create a 30-day plan draft and share it with people-ops and the assigned buddy.
- Add new-hire to team calendar invites for orientation, team retro, and product demo.
- Assign a technical buddy and schedule a 90-minute Day-1 deep-dive.

People-ops responsibilities (pre-start):
- Send welcome packet (logistics, benefits links, tax forms) and first-week checklist.
- Kick off security/compliance training enrollment (must-complete by day 7).
- Coordinate relocation or remote-stipend logistics if applicable.

Technical buddy responsibilities (pre-start):
- Verify onboarding repo links, runbook access, and recommended reading list are up to date.
- Prepare starter ticket (low-risk bug or doc edit) for day 7–14.

2) 30/60/90/180 milestone plan and acceptance criteria
-----------------------------------------------------
Milestone: Day 0–30 (Orientation & Setup)
- Goals:
  - Full network and repo access, CI credentials, and IDE toolchain configured.
  - Complete security/compliance training and basic infra walkthrough.
  - Submit first non-production PR (doc, small bugfix).
- Acceptance criteria:
  - All required accounts active and verified in onboarding tracker.
  - Buddy confirms first PR merged or constructive feedback given.
  - Day-30 pulse survey returned.

Milestone: Day 31–60 (Contribution & Collaboration)
- Goals:
  - Independently own one small user-facing route or feature bug.
  - Present a short demo at a team sync.
  - First 1:1 reflection with manager resulting in a development plan.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Demonstrated pipeline from local dev to CI to staging.
  - Code review feedback incorporated and peer-reviewed by two teammates.

Milestone: Day 61–90 (Autonomy & Impact)
- Goals:
  - Deliver a scoped feature or optimization from spec to merged PR with ownership of rollout plan.
  - Begin pairing on cross-team work or integrations.
  - Establish recurring 1:1 rhythm and career conversation.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Manager and buddy independently endorse "role-ready" for routine tasks using the role-readiness rubric.
  - Positive signals in telemetry (reduced review turnaround, increased commit independence).

Milestone: Day 91–180 (Cross-team scope & Leadership foundations)
- Goals:
  - Lead a small cross-functional initiative or reduce a documented production pain point by >25% in latency/cost/error rate.
  - Mentor a new onboarder or run a knowledge-sharing session.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Evidence of broader ownership in roadmap tickets and architecture notes.
  - Promotion readiness floor met for senior hires (if applicable).

3) Manager and buddy responsibilities (detailed)
------------------------------------------------
Manager (ongoing):
- Create and update the 30/60/90/180 plan within the team onboarding template.
- Hold weekly 1:1s (day 7–90 recommended cadence) aimed at removing blockers and calibrating expectations.
- Run a formal 90-day readout: document outcomes, learning, and next-phase goals.
- Provide immediate, concrete feedback within 48 hours of any missed acceptance criteria.

Buddy (ongoing):
- Serve as the first escalation for day-to-day technical questions for first 90 days.
- Provide guided code reviews and pair-program weekly for first 30 days.
- Validate completion of checklist items and mark acceptance criteria as met in the onboarding tracker.

4) Onboarding task checklist (to be tracked in the onboarding tracker)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Technical access:
- Cloud console access (AWS/GCP) – IAM role noted
- Repo access (git, monorepo path), commit signing configured
- CI credentials and developer sandbox created
- Local stack and infrastructure automation run (script: scripts/bootstrap.sh)

Security & compliance:
- Complete security training module 1 and 2 (enrollment link)
- Sign data handling and NDA acknowledgements
- Register device with endpoint management (MDM/HSM)

Operational & product:
- Join product channel(s), read product PRD and current roadmap summary
- Shadow customer-support queue for two 2-hour blocks during week 2

People & culture:
- Schedule intro 1:1s with cross-functional partners (SRE, Product, Sales)
- Review company values and code of conduct; complete the culture exercise in the welcome packet

5) Performance & feedback cadence and templates
-----------------------------------------------
Recommended cadence:
- Weekly manager 1:1s (first 90 days direct focus on ramp impediments)
- Bi-weekly buddy syncs (peer feedback and pairing)
- Day-30 and Day-90 structured pulse surveys (people-ops) with automated reminders
- 90-day readout meeting: manager, buddy, people-ops partner present

Feedback templates (short form):
- "Observed behavior": [concrete example]
- "Impact": [user/product/infra/people impact]
- "Suggested next step": [concrete, time-bound suggestion]

Calibrations:
- At day 90, the manager completes a role-readiness rubric and shares it with hiring manager and people-ops for calibration when cross-team work was expected.

6) Role-readiness rubric and evidence guidelines
------------------------------------------------
Rubric dimensions (each rated 1–5):
- Technical competence (coding, architecture patterns)
- Independent ownership (can deliver scoped pieces end-to-end)
- Collaboration (PR reviews, cross-team communication)
- Reliability (on-call readiness, SLA understanding)
- Growth mindset (incorporates feedback, learning cadence)

Evidence examples (accepted artifacts):
- Merged PRs with clear changelogs and test coverage
- Architecture notes or ADR contributions
- Post-release monitoring dashboards showing reduced error trends
- Peer feedback excerpts from 2+ teammates

7) Accommodations, escalations, and remediation paths
-----------------------------------------------------
Reasonable accommodations:
- People-ops coordinates accommodations for disability access, adjusted start dates, or equipment needs. Managers must not ask for medical details; direct to people-ops.

Escalation levels:
- Level 1 (team-level): manager + buddy; target resolution 5 working days.
- Level 2 (people-ops involvement): People-ops partner coordinates and documents adjustments; target 10 working days.
- Level 3 (formal performance remediation): Initiate Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) per performance policy with HR/legal review; see templates in related pages.

8) Metrics, dashboards, and quarterly review triggers
----------------------------------------------------
Primary metrics to track (dashboard maintained by people-ops):
- Time-to-autonomy distribution (median, 75th percentile) broken down by role and seniority
- Day-30 and Day-90 NPS and qualitative themes
- Onboarding checklist completion rate by day 7 and day 30
- New-hire bug/rollback rate for first 90 days (normalized by team size)

Quarterly triggers:
- If median TTA > target for two consecutive quarters for a hiring cohort, trigger a cross-functional onboarding retro and remediation plan.

9) Templates and links (appendix)
----------------------------------
- 30/60/90 plan template: /confluence/templates/30-60-90-onboarding-template
- Onboarding tracker: internal spreadsheet link (private)
- New-hire PR starter ticket template: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATES/onboarding.md
- Security training enrollment: /learning/security/mandatory
- Equipment request form: /it/equipment-requests

Appendix A: Example 90-day readout agenda
-----------------------------------------
1. 5 min - Manager opening: objectives and current state
2. 10 min - New hire demo: show what was built or improved
3. 10 min - Buddy observations and evidence (PRs, dashboards)
4. 15 min - Manager and new-hire calibration on next-phase goals
5. 10 min - People-ops notes and final acceptance status ("On track", "Needs support", "Remediation")
6. Action items and owners (published to the onboarding tracker)

Appendix B: Quick-start checklist for remote hires (first 72 hours)
------------------------------------------------------------------
- Day 0: Welcome packet, account activation, schedule 1:1 with manager and buddy (30m each)
- Day 1: Orientation session, MDM enrollment, run bootstrap.sh with buddy over Zoom
- Day 2–3: Take starter ticket, join product demo, complete security module 1

Ownership and maintenance
------------------------
- Primary owner: people-ops (onboarding program) – Ava Chen
- Secondary owners: hiring manager and technical buddy assigned per hire
- Review cadence: this blueprint must be reviewed annually or after any major changes to the platform onboarding flow (e.g., new IAM model, VPC onboarding changes).

Feedback on this blueprint
-------------------------
Send suggestions or improvement requests to people-ops-feedback@redwood.ai with subject: "Onboarding blueprint feedback". Significant changes require reviewer sign-off listed at the top of this page.

End of document

source.title: probation-acceleration-and-360-development-guidelines-2026
source.author: Nina Ortega
source.content:
Overview
======

Purpose: This document defines Redwood's Probation Acceleration Program (PAP) and the structured 360° development feedback cycle used during the first 120 days of employment. The goal is to support new hires (ICs and new managers) with rapid clarity on expectations, early capability development, and fair, calibrated assessment at the end of probation.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all full-time and fixed-term hires (individual contributors and managers) in global teams, excluding contractors on < 90-day engagements.
- Covers the first 120 calendar days from start date: 30/60/90 checkpoints and an optional 120-day stretch review for high-impact roles.

Principles
----------
- Transparent expectations: Every hire receives a role-specific success plan before Day 1.
- Frequent, documented touchpoints: Weekly 1:1s plus formal checkpoint notes at 30/60/90 days.
- Development-first assessment: Feedback is coaching-oriented; rating is a calibrated outcome of evidence.
- Multi-source input: Manager, peers, cross-functional partners, and self-assessment form the 360°.
- Time-bound remediation: Clear improvement windows and escalation paths if expectations are not met.

Program overview
----------------
1. Preboarding (Day -7 to Day 0)
   - People Ops sends role success plan and first-30-day trajectory template.
   - Manager confirms onboarding buddy and schedules first-week syncs.

2. Early ramp (Day 0–30) — Focus: Learn & integrate
   - Deliverables: environment access, basic contributor tasks, two cross-team introductions.
   - Checkpoint: 30-day note submitted by manager with self-assessment attached.

3. Competency build (Day 31–60) — Focus: independent delivery
   - Deliverables: at least one scoped deliverable, initial impact metrics, participation in team rituals.
   - Checkpoint: 60-day note (manager + buddy + peer highlights).

4. Acceleration & assessment (Day 61–90) — Focus: consistent performance
   - Deliverables: repeatable contribution, ownership of a small area, demonstrated cultural fit.
   - 90-day formal 360: collate manager assessment, peer feedback, self-review.

5. Optional extension (Day 91–120)
   - Triggered when role complexity warrants more evidence. If used, an explicit improvement plan and success criteria are documented.

Roles and responsibilities
--------------------------
- Hiring Manager:
  - Drafts role success plan (preboarding), holds weekly 1:1s, completes checkpoint notes, owns final probation recommendation.
- Onboarding Buddy:
  - Day-to-day support, documents practical blockers, provides peer input at checkpoints.
- People Ops:
  - Sends templates, ensures HR compliance, coordinates 360 collators, logs final outcome.
- Peer Reviewers (2–4):
  - Provide behavioral/working-style feedback and examples; must be a recent collaborator.

30/60/90 Checkpoint template (to be used in Confluence page)
-----------------------------------------------------------
- Status summary (1–3 sentences)
- Key accomplishments (bullet list)
- Observed gaps and blocker list
- Development goals for next period (SMART)
- Manager recommendation (on-track / needs-extension / off-track)

360° feedback process (formalized)
--------------------------------
1. Trigger: 90-day checkpoint (or 120-day end if extended).
2. Collation window: People Ops opens a 7-day window to collect feedback via the standard form.
3. Required inputs: manager assessment, self-review, 2–4 peer reviews (one cross-functional if applicable), onboarding buddy note.
4. Anonymity: Peer comments are aggregated and de-identified for coaching use. Ratings are visible to People Ops and the manager.
5. Outcome meeting: Manager + People Ops + employee meet to discuss results and agree next steps.

Calibration and scoring rubric
----------------------------
| Competency area | 3 — Meets expectations | 2 — Developing | 1 — Needs support | Evidence examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Role delivery | Consistently delivers scoped work with quality and on time. | Delivers with support; quality inconsistent. | Repeated missed deliverables or quality gaps. | PRs merged, bug rate, demo outcomes. |
| Collaboration | Communicates clearly; proactively aligns with partners. | Responsive; sometimes misses cross-team context. | Reactive, misses updates or creates rework. | Meeting notes, peer examples. |
| Learning velocity | Rapidly ramps; applies feedback within cycle. | Learns steadily but needs guidance. | Slow uptake; repeated same feedback. | Task completion timelines, quiz/knowledge checks. |
| Ownership & judgment | Takes responsibility; proposes improvements. | Needs nudges to own tasks; good escalation. | Avoids decisions; unclear escalation. | Decision logs, project outcomes. |

Scoring and decision logic
- Aggregate score >= 10 (of 12): On-track — convert to regular employment.
- Aggregate 7–9: Extension to Day 120 with agreed improvement plan.
- Aggregate <= 6: Off-track — initiate formal performance process (HR-led) with right-to-appeal.

Manager checklist (pre-90 day)
-----------------------------
- [ ] Role success plan exists and was shared pre-Day 1.
- [ ] Weekly 1:1s scheduled and notes captured.
- [ ] Buddy assigned and check-ins recorded.
- [ ] Two peer reviewers identified and invited.
- [ ] 30/60 checkpoint notes completed on time.
- [ ] 90-day 360 request opened with People Ops.
- [ ] Calibration review scheduled with People Ops if extension or off-track.

HR and operational steps (People Ops)
-----------------------------------
1. Create candidate's probation file at offer acceptance; attach role success plan.
2. Day 7: Verify access and initial pulse check with manager.
3. Day 30/60/90: Reminder emails + collate templates.
4. On 90-day close: run automatic 360 collator, produce anonymized summary for coaching.
5. Archive final decision and follow-up plan in HRIS (Workday/Gusto) within 5 business days.

Templates & sample language
--------------------------
- Invitation to peer reviewer (email):
  "Hi <Name>, please provide a 5–8 sentence collaboration summary and 1–2 examples of working behaviors for <New hire>. Deadline: <date>. Feedback will be aggregated"

- Manager development note starter:
  "<Name> started on <date>. Strengths observed: ... Areas to focus: ... Proposed target for next 30 days: ..."

Metrics & success criteria
------------------------
- Time-to-decision: 90% of probation decisions completed within 5 business days after the 90-day meeting.
- Calibration variance: less than 10% variance in recommendations for equivalent roles after quarterly calibration.
- Ramp-effectiveness: increase in 6-month new-hire contribution (measured as % of expected deliverables) by 15% year-over-year after program rollout.

Escalation paths
----------------
- If manager recommends "off-track": People Ops convenes a calibration panel (people-ops lead + manager peer + HR business partner).
- If the employee contests the outcome: they may request a second review within 10 business days; People Ops documents the appeal and timelines.

Rollout plan & training
-----------------------
1. Q2 2026 pilot with Eng, Product, and Customer Success (10 hires).
2. Train managers (90-minute workshop) and onboarding buddies (60-minute session).
3. Collect pilot metrics and revise templates.
4. Company-wide launch targeted for Q3 2026.

Related pages
-------------
- /people-ops/onboarding-immersion-rotation-playbook-2026
- /people-ops/first-year-engagement-and-success-milestones-2028

Appendix: Quick decision flow (text diagram)
-------------------------------------------
Day 90  -->  Collect 360 inputs (7 days)  -->  Manager+People Ops meeting  -->  Decision: On-track / Extension / Off-track  -->  Action (convert / extension plan / HR process)

Document ownership and review cadence
------------------------------------
- Owner: People Ops (Nina Ortega)
- Review cadence: Annual; immediate review after pilot completion.

Contact & questions
-------------------
For questions about templates, scheduling the calibration panel, or exceptional cases, contact people-ops@redwood.com or Nina Ortega (nina.ortega@redwood.com).

   - Triggered when role complexity warrants more evidence. If used, an explicit improvement plan and success criteria are documented.

--------------------------
- Hiring Manager:
  - Drafts role success plan (preboarding), holds weekly 1:1s, completes checkpoint notes, owns final probation recommendation.
- Onboarding Buddy:
  - Day-to-day support, documents practical blockers, provides peer input at checkpoints.
- People Ops:
  - Sends templates, ensures HR compliance, coordinates 360 collators, logs final outcome.
- Peer Reviewers (2–4):
  - Provide behavioral/working-style feedback and examples; must be a recent collaborator.

30/60/90 Checkpoint template (to be used in Confluence page)
-----------------------------------------------------------
- Status summary (1–3 sentences)
- Key accomplishments (bullet list)
- Observed gaps and blocker list
- Development goals for next period (SMART)
- Manager recommendation (on-track / needs-extension / off-track)

360° feedback process (formalized)
--------------------------------
1. Trigger: 90-day checkpoint (or 120-day end if extended).

3. Required inputs: manager assessment, self-review, 2–4 peer reviews (one cross-functional if applicable), onboarding buddy note.

| Competency area | 3 — Meets expectations | 2 — Developing | 1 — Needs support | Evidence examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Role delivery | Consistently delivers scoped work with quality and on time. | Delivers with support; quality inconsistent. | Repeated missed deliverables or quality gaps. | PRs merged, bug rate, demo outcomes. |

-----------------------------

- [ ] Weekly 1:1s scheduled and notes captured.
- [ ] Buddy assigned and check-ins recorded.
- [ ] Two peer reviewers identified and invited.
- [ ] 30/60 checkpoint notes completed on time.
- [ ] 90-day 360 request opened with People Ops.
- [ ] Calibration review scheduled with People Ops if extension or off-track.

-----------------------------------
1. Create candidate's probation file at offer acceptance; attach role success plan.
2. Day 7: Verify access and initial pulse check with manager.
3. Day 30/60/90: Reminder emails + collate templates.

--------------------------
- Invitation to peer reviewer (email):

- Manager development note starter:
  "<Name> started on <date>. Strengths observed: ... Areas to focus: ... Proposed target for next 30 days: ..."

Metrics & success criteria
------------------------
- Time-to-decision: 90% of probation decisions completed within 5 business days after the 90-day meeting.
- Calibration variance: less than 10% variance in recommendations for equivalent roles after quarterly calibration.
- Ramp-effectiveness: increase in 6-month new-hire contribution (measured as % of expected deliverables) by 15% year-over-year after program rollout.

------------------------------------

source.content:
Purpose
-------
This guide defines a lightweight, repeatable approach for managers and people-ops to run short, outcome-oriented performance cycles called "performance sprints". The goal is to increase feedback frequency, reduce ambiguity in expectations, and create actionable development plans aligned to team priorities.

Scope
-----
Applies to all managers and ICs at Redwood Inference. The approach is intended to complement annual performance reviews, not replace them. Use performance sprints for: onboarding checkpoints, quarterly goal pulses, skill development pushes, and short-term performance recovery.

Principles
----------
- Frequent: shorter cycles (4–8 weeks) make adjustments predictable and lower-stakes.
- Transparent: shared goals, measurable outcomes, and documented follow-ups.
- Coaching-first: emphasize growth; corrective steps should escalate only when coaching fails.
- Evidence-based: base assessments on observable outcomes and examples.

High-level cadence (recommended)
-------------------------------
1. Sprint planning (week 0): manager + contributor agree 2–3 outcomes and signals of success.
2. Weekly 1:1 syncs (15–30m): focused on blockers and evidence collection.
3. Mid-sprint check (week 2 or week 4): short calibration with peer or skip-level if needed.
4. Sprint review (final week): document results, decide next steps (continue, scale, adjust, or initiate improvement plan).

Roles & ownership
------------------
- Manager: owns sprint framing, weekly 1:1 facilitation, evidence collection, and final decision.
- Contributor: co-owns outcomes, provides status updates, and curates evidence/artifacts.
- People Ops: provides templates, moderates calibration sessions, and ensures consistency across teams.
- Skip-level/Peer Reviewer: optional reviewer during mid-sprint check for cross-team context.

Sprint Planning Template (to document in HRIS or shared doc)
-------------------------------------------------------------
- Sprint title:
- Start / end dates:
- Priority (onboarding / growth / execution / recovery):
- 2–3 outcomes (headline + measurable signal of success):
  1. Outcome A — success if: [metric / deliverable / behavior].
  2. Outcome B — success if: [metric / deliverable / behavior].
- Key blockers & support requested:
- Checkpoints (dates for mid-sprint, artifacts):

Weekly 1:1 structure (15–30 minutes)
-----------------------------------
1. Quick pulse (1–2 min): energy + focus indicator.
2. Evidence update (3–6 min): concrete progress, links to PRs, docs, demo recordings.
3. Blockers & rapid decisions (5–10 min): what manager will unblock this week.
4. Development micro-task (2–5 min): one small growth activity (read, pairing, refactor).
5. Calendar & next steps (1 min).

Evidence examples (acceptable artifacts)
--------------------------------------
- Merged PRs with release notes
- Demo video or RFC with reviewer comments
- Customer feedback or bug triage tickets closed
- Quantitative signals (latency reduction %, cost/unit change, embeddings accuracy delta)

Calibration rubric (use for consistency in manager assessments)
-------------------------------------------------------------
| Dimension | Exceeds expectations | Meets expectations | Needs improvement |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Outcomes  | Delivers reliably and drives additional value | Delivers agreed outcomes on time | Misses multiple outcomes or delivers poor quality |
| Collaboration | Proactively aligns, mentors peers, and removes blockers | Works well within team and communicates updates | Frequent friction, missed handoffs |
| Craft & Execution | Produces high-quality artifacts; anticipates issues | Completes work with acceptable quality | Rework/bugs or repeated missed acceptance criteria |
| Growth mindset | Seeks feedback and applies learning quickly | Accepts feedback and shows progress | Resistant to feedback or slow to change |

Manager assessment guidance
--------------------------
- Use concrete examples (link to artifacts). Avoid vague phrases like "needs to improve communication" without situational evidence.
- Prefer patterns over single incidents. Document 2–3 representative examples per dimension.
- When rating "needs improvement": capture specific behaviors, past coaching attempts, and proposed manager support.

Promotion readiness checklist (for ICs)
--------------------------------------
- Clear impact on product or platform (examples & metrics)
- Cross-team influence documented (RFCs, shared libs, stakeholder testimonials)
- Demonstrated leadership behaviors (mentorship, design ownership)
- Sustained performance across 2+ sprints and peer calibration
- Manager endorsement + documented development gaps and plan

Progression outcomes and next steps
----------------------------------
- Continue: outcomes met; prepare for next sprint with escalated scope.
- Scale: exceeded outcomes; identify stretch assignments and potential promotion timeline.
- Adjust: partial success; run a follow-up sprint with revised outcomes.
- Improvement Plan: significant gaps after coaching; open a 30–60–90 improvement plan (see flow).

Improvement Plan flow (30–60–90)
--------------------------------
1. Trigger: Two consecutive sprints rated as "needs improvement" or a single severe incident.
2. Draft plan: manager + people-ops co-author clear expectations and timeline (30/60/90 days).
3. Weekly progress notes: short updates in HRIS and 1:1s.
4. Midpoint review (30 days): determine if sufficient progress.
5. Final review (60/90 days): decide next step (return to normal cadence, extend plan, role change, or separation).

Conversation frameworks (phrases and structure)
---------------------------------------------
- Situation-Behavior-Impact-Request (SBIR):
  1. Situation: "In last sprint during the API rollout..."
  2. Behavior: "I noticed the PR lacked test coverage and missing schema docs."
  3. Impact: "This led to a rollback and customer downtime for 2 hours."
  4. Request: "Can we agree on a pre-merge checklist for the next release?"
- Radical candor prompts: "I'm invested in your success" before delivering hard feedback.

Onboarding checkpoints aligned to sprints (new hire first 90 days)
----------------------------------------------------------------
- Sprint 1 (week 0–4): Setup, first small deliverable, buddy pairing, team intro presentation.
- Sprint 2 (week 5–8): Ownership of a non-critical feature or infra task, demonstrate code review competency.
- Sprint 3 (week 9–12): Lead a small project, show cross-team collaboration, present outcomes to stakeholders.

Manager toolkit (templates & commands)
-------------------------------------
- Sprint planning doc template: use /templates/perf-sprint-template in the shared drive.
- HRIS: create sprint record: CLI example (internal HR tool):
  curl -X POST https://hr.internal.redwood/v1/sprints -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"owner":"<manager>","contributor":"<name>","start":"YYYY-MM-DD","end":"YYYY-MM-DD","outcomes":["..."]}'
- Weekly note snippet (paste to HRIS or PR comment): "Week 2: shipping on track; unblocked by infra review. Artifacts: PR#123, demo.mp4"

Privacy & legal notes
---------------------
- Keep all sprint documentation in Redwood internal systems only. Do not post personal performance notes in public repos.
- For disciplinary actions or PIPs that may affect employment status, loop in People Ops and Legal before finalizing.

Frequently asked questions
--------------------------
Q: How do sprints interact with annual reviews?
A: Performance sprints generate the evidence set that managers use for annual calibration. They should be referenced in the review narrative.

Q: Can an IC request a sprint?
A: Yes — contributors may ask for a sprint to accelerate development or clarify expectations; manager approval required.

Q: What if a manager is overloaded and cannot run weekly 1:1s?
A: Prioritize 1:1s; if not possible, schedule a 20m replaceable sync and seek peer/skip-level touchpoints to ensure continuity. Inform People Ops if sustained backlog occurs.

Appendix: sample sprint review note (copy into HRIS)
--------------------------------------------------
Sprint: Reduce inference tail latency
Dates: 2026-01-05 to 2026-02-02
Outcomes:
 - Outcome 1: Identify and close top 3 latency hotspots — SUCCESS (PR#452, benchmark report link)
 - Outcome 2: Ship LRU cache micro-optimizations — PARTIAL (deployed behind flag, 8% improvement in p95)
Manager assessment:
 - Outcomes: Meets expectations
 - Collaboration: Exceeds expectations (led cross-team incident simulation)
 - Growth: Demonstrated learning in kernel-level perf analysis
Next steps:
 - Follow-up sprint to roll out cache to production with rollout plan; mentor pairing on kernel profiling.

Related resources
-----------------
- /confluence/people-ops/onboarding-checklist-2025
- /confluence/people-ops/promotion-guidelines-2024
- /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/slo-onboarding

Owner & contacts
----------------
People Ops (Lead): Priya Natarajan — people-ops@redwood.ai
Manager lead: Aisha Khan — aisha@redwood.ai

Change history
--------------
- 2025-11-10 — Created (Aisha Khan)
- 2026-01-15 — Updated calibration rubric and PIP flow (Miguel Alvarez)
- 2026-02-20 — Finalized templates and CLI examples; publish (Aisha Khan)

text:
Purpose
-------
This guide defines a lightweight, repeatable approach for managers and people-ops to run short, outcome-oriented performance cycles called "performance sprints". The goal is to increase feedback frequency, reduce ambiguity in expectations, and create actionable development plans aligned to team priorities.

Scope
-----
Applies to all managers and ICs at Redwood Inference. The approach is intended to complement annual performance reviews, not replace them. Use performance sprints for: onboarding checkpoints, quarterly goal pulses, skill development pushes, and short-term performance recovery.

Principles
----------
- Frequent: shorter cycles (4–8 weeks) make adjustments predictable and lower-stakes.
- Transparent: shared goals, measurable outcomes, and documented follow-ups.
- Coaching-first: emphasize growth; corrective steps should escalate only when coaching fails.
- Evidence-based: base assessments on observable outcomes and examples.

High-level cadence (recommended)
-------------------------------
1. Sprint planning (week 0): manager + contributor agree 2–3 outcomes and signals of success.
2. Weekly 1:1 syncs (15–30m): focused on blockers and evidence collection.
3. Mid-sprint check (week 2 or week 4): short calibration with peer or skip-level if needed.
4. Sprint review (final week): document results, decide next steps (continue, scale, adjust, or initiate improvement plan).

Roles & ownership
------------------
- Manager: owns sprint framing, weekly 1:1 facilitation, evidence collection, and final decision.
- Contributor: co-owns outcomes, provides status updates, and curates evidence/artifacts.
- People Ops: provides templates, moderates calibration sessions, and ensures consistency across teams.
- Skip-level/Peer Reviewer: optional reviewer during mid-sprint check for cross-team context.

Sprint Planning Template (to document in HRIS or shared doc)
-------------------------------------------------------------
- Sprint title:
- Start / end dates:
- Priority (onboarding / growth / execution / recovery):
- 2–3 outcomes (headline + measurable signal of success):
  1. Outcome A — success if: [metric / deliverable / behavior].
  2. Outcome B — success if: [metric / deliverable / behavior].
- Key blockers & support requested:
- Checkpoints (dates for mid-sprint, artifacts):

Weekly 1:1 structure (15–30 minutes)
-----------------------------------
1. Quick pulse (1–2 min): energy + focus indicator.
2. Evidence update (3–6 min): concrete progress, links to PRs, docs, demo recordings.
3. Blockers & rapid decisions (5–10 min): what manager will unblock this week.
4. Development micro-task (2–5 min): one small growth activity (read, pairing, refactor).
5. Calendar & next steps (1 min).

Evidence examples (acceptable artifacts)
--------------------------------------
- Merged PRs with release notes
- Demo video or RFC with reviewer comments
- Customer feedback or bug triage tickets closed
- Quantitative signals (latency reduction %, cost/unit change, embeddings accuracy delta)

Calibration rubric (use for consistency in manager assessments)
-------------------------------------------------------------
| Dimension | Exceeds expectations | Meets expectations | Needs improvement |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Outcomes  | Delivers reliably and drives additional value | Delivers agreed outcomes on time | Misses multiple outcomes or delivers poor quality |
| Collaboration | Proactively aligns, mentors peers, and removes blockers | Works well within team and communicates updates | Frequent friction, missed handoffs |
| Craft & Execution | Produces high-quality artifacts; anticipates issues | Completes work with acceptable quality | Rework/bugs or repeated missed acceptance criteria |
| Growth mindset | Seeks feedback and applies learning quickly | Accepts feedback and shows progress | Resistant to feedback or slow to change |

Manager assessment guidance
--------------------------
- Use concrete examples (link to artifacts). Avoid vague phrases like "needs to improve communication" without situational evidence.
- Prefer patterns over single incidents. Document 2–3 representative examples per dimension.
- When rating "needs improvement": capture specific behaviors, past coaching attempts, and proposed manager support.

Promotion readiness checklist (for ICs)
--------------------------------------
- Clear impact on product or platform (examples & metrics)
- Cross-team influence documented (RFCs, shared libs, stakeholder testimonials)
- Demonstrated leadership behaviors (mentorship, design ownership)
- Sustained performance across 2+ sprints and peer calibration
- Manager endorsement + documented development gaps and plan

Progression outcomes and next steps
----------------------------------
- Continue: outcomes met; prepare for next sprint with escalated scope.
- Scale: exceeded outcomes; identify stretch assignments and potential promotion timeline.
- Adjust: partial success; run a follow-up sprint with revised outcomes.
- Improvement Plan: significant gaps after coaching; open a 30–60–90 improvement plan (see flow).

Improvement Plan flow (30–60–90)
--------------------------------
1. Trigger: Two consecutive sprints rated as "needs improvement" or a single severe incident.
2. Draft plan: manager + people-ops co-author clear expectations and timeline (30/60/90 days).
3. Weekly progress notes: short updates in HRIS and 1:1s.
4. Midpoint review (30 days): determine if sufficient progress.
5. Final review (60/90 days): decide next step (return to normal cadence, extend plan, role change, or separation).

Conversation frameworks (phrases and structure)
---------------------------------------------
- Situation-Behavior-Impact-Request (SBIR):
  1. Situation: "In last sprint during the API rollout..."
  2. Behavior: "I noticed the PR lacked test coverage and missing schema docs."
  3. Impact: "This led to a rollback and customer downtime for 2 hours."
  4. Request: "Can we agree on a pre-merge checklist for the next release?"
- Radical candor prompts: "I'm invested in your success" before delivering hard feedback.

Onboarding checkpoints aligned to sprints (new hire first 90 days)
----------------------------------------------------------------
- Sprint 1 (week 0–4): Setup, first small deliverable, buddy pairing, team intro presentation.
- Sprint 2 (week 5–8): Ownership of a non-critical feature or infra task, demonstrate code review competency.
- Sprint 3 (week 9–12): Lead a small project, show cross-team collaboration, present outcomes to stakeholders.

Manager toolkit (templates & commands)
-------------------------------------
- Sprint planning doc template: use /templates/perf-sprint-template in the shared drive.
- HRIS: create sprint record: CLI example (internal HR tool):
  curl -X POST https://hr.internal.redwood/v1/sprints -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"owner":"<manager>","contributor":"<name>","start":"YYYY-MM-DD","end":"YYYY-MM-DD","outcomes":["..."]}'
- Weekly note snippet (paste to HRIS or PR comment): "Week 2: shipping on track; unblocked by infra review. Artifacts: PR#123, demo.mp4"

Privacy & legal notes
---------------------
- Keep all sprint documentation in Redwood internal systems only. Do not post personal performance notes in public repos.
- For disciplinary actions or PIPs that may affect employment status, loop in People Ops and Legal before finalizing.

Frequently asked questions
--------------------------
Q: How do sprints interact with annual reviews?
A: Performance sprints generate the evidence set that managers use for annual calibration. They should be referenced in the review narrative.

Q: Can an IC request a sprint?
A: Yes — contributors may ask for a sprint to accelerate development or clarify expectations; manager approval required.

Q: What if a manager is overloaded and cannot run weekly 1:1s?
A: Prioritize 1:1s; if not possible, schedule a 20m replaceable sync and seek peer/skip-level touchpoints to ensure continuity. Inform People Ops if sustained backlog occurs.

Appendix: sample sprint review note (copy into HRIS)
--------------------------------------------------
Sprint: Reduce inference tail latency
Dates: 2026-01-05 to 2026-02-02
Outcomes:
 - Outcome 1: Identify and close top 3 latency hotspots — SUCCESS (PR#452, benchmark report link)
 - Outcome 2: Ship LRU cache micro-optimizations — PARTIAL (deployed behind flag, 8% improvement in p95)
Manager assessment:
 - Outcomes: Meets expectations
 - Collaboration: Exceeds expectations (led cross-team incident simulation)
 - Growth: Demonstrated learning in kernel-level perf analysis
Next steps:
 - Follow-up sprint to roll out cache to production with rollout plan; mentor pairing on kernel profiling.

Related resources
-----------------
- /confluence/people-ops/onboarding-checklist-2025
- /confluence/people-ops/promotion-guidelines-2024
- /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/slo-onboarding

Owner & contacts
----------------
People Ops (Lead): Priya Natarajan — people-ops@redwood.ai
Manager lead: Aisha Khan — aisha@redwood.ai

--------------
- 2025-11-10 — Created (Aisha Khan)
- 2026-01-15 — Updated calibration rubric and PIP flow (Miguel Alvarez)
- 2026-02-20 — Finalized templates and CLI examples; publish (Aisha Khan)
